title: Cat Vomiting Undigested Food After Eating: Causes and Care Guide
meta_desc: Learn why your cat is throwing up undigested food, if it's an emergency, and how taking a photo can help your vet with a faster diagnosis.
content_html:
TL;DR: Regurgitating undigested food is often caused by eating too fast, but persistent episodes or accompanying symptoms like lethargy require veterinary attention. Taking a photo helps your vet distinguish between regurgitation and vomiting to determine the urgency of care.

What does it mean when my cat regurgitates undigested food?

When your cat ejects food that is still in its original shape shortly after a meal, it is often 'regurgitation' rather than true vomiting. This occurs when the food has not yet reached the stomach or has been rejected immediately due to eating too quickly, a behavior often called 'scarf and barf.' However, it can also be a sign of esophageal issues or food sensitivities.

Is my cat's regurgitation a medical emergency?

  • The urgency level for this symptom is Medium.
  • If this happens only once and your cat remains active and alert, it may not be an immediate crisis.
  • Contact your veterinarian promptly if the behavior persists for more than 24 hours.
  • Seek medical attention if regurgitation is accompanied by lethargy, diarrhea, or a refusal to drink water to rule out obstructions or systemic illness.

How does a photo of the regurgitated food help my veterinarian?

  • Capture a photo of the undigested food and the surrounding liquid as a tool for your veterinary team.
  • Visual evidence allows the vet to determine the presence of bile, blood, or foreign objects.
  • Photos help distinguish between simple regurgitation and active vomiting to determine how quickly your pet needs to be seen.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Vomiting in cats may result from dietary indiscretion, parasitic infection, bacterial or viral gastroenteritis, anxiety, or motion sickness. Initial treatment involves withholding food for a short period and then feeding an easily digestible diet, offering small amounts of water frequently. Careful monitoring for persistent vomiting, depression, abdominal discomfort, and fever is critical, and reevaluation is warranted if vomiting persists. Examination of the oral cavity should be performed, looking for evidence of yarn, thread, or needles, and abdominal palpation should assess for foreign bodies or abdominal discomfort. Radiographs can identify radiopaque foreign objects or signs of intestinal distention indicating possible obstruction. Chronic vomiting may indicate underlying thyroid, liver, or kidney dysfunction and should be investigated. For acute gastritis, small amounts of oral fluids can be given frequently, increasing the volume as vomiting subsides; ice can be provided initially as the only water source. If dehydration is moderate to severe, IV fluid therapy and a more extensive diagnostic evaluation are indicated.

Chapter: Gastroenterology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 390)

faqs.question: Can I use a slow-feeder bowl to stop this?
faqs.answer: Yes, if the cause is eating too quickly, a slow-feeder bowl or puzzle feeder can help regulate their pace and reduce regurgitation episodes.

faqs.question: When should I call an emergency vet?
faqs.answer: Call an emergency vet immediately if your cat is retching without producing anything, has a bloated abdomen, or appears to be in significant pain.

faqs.question: Is undigested food different from hairballs?
faqs.answer: Yes, undigested food is usually a tubular shape of kibble or wet food, whereas hairballs consist of tightly packed fur and digestive juices.
